quoteText:
Well,
Harry, what brings you out so early? I
thought you dandies never got up till two, and were not visible till
five.
subQuotationList:
['Well,
Harry,', 'what brings you out so early? I
thought you dandies never got up till two, and were not visible till
five.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
Pure family affection, I assure you, Uncle George. I want to get
something out of you.
subQuotationList: ['Pure family affection, I assure you, Uncle George. I want to get\nsomething out of you.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
Money, I suppose, Well, sit
down and tell me all about it. Young people, nowadays, imagine that
money is everything.
subQuotationList: ['Money, I suppose,', 'Well, sit\ndown and tell me all about it. Young people, nowadays, imagine that\nmoney is everything.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
Yes, and
when they grow older they know it. But I don t want money. It is only
people who pay their bills who want that, Uncle George, and I never pay
mine. Credit is the capital of a younger son, and one lives charmingly
upon it. Besides, I always deal with Dartmoor s tradesmen, and
consequently they never bother me. What I want is information: not
useful information, of course; useless information.
subQuotationList:
['Yes,', 'and
when they grow older they know it. But I don t want money. It is only
people who pay their bills who want that, Uncle George, and I never pay
mine. Credit is the capital of a younger son, and one lives charmingly
upon it. Besides, I always deal with Dartmoor s tradesmen, and
consequently they never bother me. What I want is information: not
useful information, of course; useless information.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
Well, I can tell you anything that is in an English Blue Book, Harry,
although those fellows nowadays write a lot of nonsense. When I was in
the Diplomatic, things were much better. But I hear they let them in
now by examination. What can you expect? Examinations, sir, are pure
humbug from beginning to end. If a man is a gentleman, he knows quite
enough, and if he is not a gentleman, whatever he knows is bad for
him.
subQuotationList:
['Well, I can tell you anything that is in an English Blue Book, Harry,
although those fellows nowadays write a lot of nonsense. When I was in
the Diplomatic, things were much better. But I hear they let them in
now by examination. What can you expect? Examinations, sir, are pure
humbug from beginning to end. If a man is a gentleman, he knows quite
enough, and if he is not a gentleman, whatever he knows is bad for
him.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText: Mr. Dorian Gray does not belong to Blue Books, Uncle George,
subQuotationList: ['Mr. Dorian Gray does not belong to Blue Books, Uncle George,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: Mr. Dorian Gray? Who is he?
subQuotationList: ['Mr. Dorian Gray? Who is he?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
That is what I have come to learn, Uncle George. Or rather, I know who
he is. He is the last Lord Kelso s grandson. His mother was a Devereux,
Lady Margaret Devereux. I want you to tell me about his mother. What
was she like? Whom did she marry? You have known nearly everybody in
your time, so you might have known her. I am very much interested in
Mr. Gray at present. I have only just met him.
subQuotationList:
['That is what I have come to learn, Uncle George. Or rather, I know who
he is. He is the last Lord Kelso s grandson. His mother was a Devereux,
Lady Margaret Devereux. I want you to tell me about his mother. What
was she like? Whom did she marry? You have known nearly everybody in
your time, so you might have known her. I am very much interested in
Mr. Gray at present. I have only just met him.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']
mentionTextsList: [['Uncle George', 'he', 'He', 'the last Lord Kelso s grandson', 'His', 'you', 'his', 'You', 'your', 'you', 'Mr. Gray', 'him']]
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Fermor'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Fermor'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Fermor'], ['Lord Fermor'], ['Lord Fermor'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText:
Kelso s grandson! Kelso s grandson! ... Of
course.... I knew his mother intimately. I believe I was at her
christening. She was an extraordinarily beautiful girl, Margaret
Devereux, and made all the men frantic by running away with a penniless
young fellow a mere nobody, sir, a subaltern in a foot regiment, or
something of that kind. Certainly. I remember the whole thing as if it
happened yesterday. The poor chap was killed in a duel at Spa a few
months after the marriage. There was an ugly story about it. They said
Kelso got some rascally adventurer, some Belgian brute, to insult his
son-in-law in public paid him, sir, to do it, paid him and that the
fellow spitted his man as if he had been a pigeon. The thing was hushed
up, but, egad, Kelso ate his chop alone at the club for some time
afterwards. He brought his daughter back with him, I was told, and she
never spoke to him again. Oh, yes; it was a bad business. The girl
died, too, died within a year. So she left a son, did she? I had
forgotten that. What sort of boy is he? If he is like his mother, he
must be a good-looking chap.
subQuotationList:
['Kelso s grandson!', 'Kelso s grandson! ... Of
course.... I knew his mother intimately. I believe I was at her
christening. She was an extraordinarily beautiful girl, Margaret
Devereux, and made all the men frantic by running away with a penniless
young fellow a mere nobody, sir, a subaltern in a foot regiment, or
something of that kind. Certainly. I remember the whole thing as if it
happened yesterday. The poor chap was killed in a duel at Spa a few
months after the marriage. There was an ugly story about it. They said
Kelso got some rascally adventurer, some Belgian brute, to insult his
son-in-law in public paid him, sir, to do it, paid him and that the
fellow spitted his man as if he had been a pigeon. The thing was hushed
up, but, egad, Kelso ate his chop alone at the club for some time
afterwards. He brought his daughter back with him, I was told, and she
never spoke to him again. Oh, yes; it was a bad business. The girl
died, too, died within a year. So she left a son, did she? I had
forgotten that. What sort of boy is he? If he is like his mother, he
must be a good-looking chap.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray']], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Henry'], ['Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: He is very good-looking,
subQuotationList: ['He is very good-looking,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
I hope he will fall into proper hands, He
should have a pot of money waiting for him if Kelso did the right thing
by him. His mother had money, too. All the Selby property came to her,
through her grandfather. Her grandfather hated Kelso, thought him a
mean dog. He was, too. Came to Madrid once when I was there. Egad, I
was ashamed of him. The Queen used to ask me about the English noble
who was always quarrelling with the cabmen about their fares. They made
quite a story of it. I didn t dare show my face at Court for a month. I
hope he treated his grandson better than he did the jarvies.
subQuotationList:
['I hope he will fall into proper hands,', 'He
should have a pot of money waiting for him if Kelso did the right thing
by him. His mother had money, too. All the Selby property came to her,
through her grandfather. Her grandfather hated Kelso, thought him a
mean dog. He was, too. Came to Madrid once when I was there. Egad, I
was ashamed of him. The Queen used to ask me about the English noble
who was always quarrelling with the cabmen about their fares. They made
quite a story of it. I didn t dare show my face at Court for a month. I
hope he treated his grandson better than he did the jarvies.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray']], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText:
I don t know, I fancy that the boy will be well
off. He is not of age yet. He has Selby, I know. He told me so. And ...
his mother was very beautiful?
subQuotationList: ['I don t know,', 'I fancy that the boy will be well\noff. He is not of age yet. He has Selby, I know. He told me so. And ...\nhis mother was very beautiful?']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
Margaret Devereux was one of the loveliest creatures I ever saw,
Harry. What on earth induced her to behave as she did, I never could
understand. She could have married anybody she chose. Carlington was
mad after her. She was romantic, though. All the women of that family
were. The men were a poor lot, but, egad! the women were wonderful.
Carlington went on his knees to her. Told me so himself. She laughed at
him, and there wasn t a girl in London at the time who wasn t after
him. And by the way, Harry, talking about silly marriages, what is this
humbug your father tells me about Dartmoor wanting to marry an
American? Ain t English girls good enough for him?
subQuotationList:
['Margaret Devereux was one of the loveliest creatures I ever saw,
Harry. What on earth induced her to behave as she did, I never could
understand. She could have married anybody she chose. Carlington was
mad after her. She was romantic, though. All the women of that family
were. The men were a poor lot, but, egad! the women were wonderful.
Carlington went on his knees to her. Told me so himself. She laughed at
him, and there wasn t a girl in London at the time who wasn t after
him. And by the way, Harry, talking about silly marriages, what is this
humbug your father tells me about Dartmoor wanting to marry an
American? Ain t English girls good enough for him?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText: It is rather fashionable to marry Americans just now, Uncle George.
subQuotationList: ['It is rather fashionable to marry Americans just now, Uncle George.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: I ll back English women against the world, Harry,
subQuotationList: ['I ll back English women against the world, Harry,']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText: The betting is on the Americans.
subQuotationList: ['The betting is on the Americans.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: They don t last, I am told,
subQuotationList: ['They don t last, I am told,']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
A long engagement exhausts them, but they are capital at a
steeplechase. They take things flying. I don t think Dartmoor has a
chance.
subQuotationList: ['A long engagement exhausts them, but they are capital at a\nsteeplechase. They take things flying. I don t think Dartmoor has a\nchance.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: Who are her people? Has she got any?
subQuotationList: ['Who are her people?', 'Has she got any?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
American girls are as clever at concealing
their parents, as English women are at concealing their past,
subQuotationList: ['American girls are as clever at concealing\ntheir parents, as English women are at concealing their past,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: They are pork-packers, I suppose?
subQuotationList: ['They are pork-packers, I suppose?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
I hope so, Uncle George, for Dartmoor s sake. I am told that
pork-packing is the most lucrative profession in America, after
politics.
subQuotationList: ['I hope so, Uncle George, for Dartmoor s sake. I am told that\npork-packing is the most lucrative profession in America, after\npolitics.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: Is she pretty?
subQuotationList: ['Is she pretty?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
She behaves as if she was beautiful. Most American women do. It is the
secret of their charm.
subQuotationList: ['She behaves as if she was beautiful. Most American women do. It is the\nsecret of their charm.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
Why can t these American women stay in their own country? They are
always telling us that it is the paradise for women.
subQuotationList: ['Why can t these American women stay in their own country? They are\nalways telling us that it is the paradise for women.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
It is. That is the reason why, like Eve, they are so excessively
anxious to get out of it, Good-bye, Uncle George. I
shall be late for lunch, if I stop any longer. Thanks for giving me the
information I wanted. I always like to know everything about my new
friends, and nothing about my old ones.
subQuotationList:
['It is. That is the reason why, like Eve, they are so excessively
anxious to get out of it,', 'Good-bye, Uncle George. I
shall be late for lunch, if I stop any longer. Thanks for giving me the
information I wanted. I always like to know everything about my new
friends, and nothing about my old ones.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: Where are you lunching, Harry?
subQuotationList: ['Where are you lunching, Harry?']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
At Aunt Agatha s. I have asked myself and Mr. Gray. He is her latest
_prot g _.
subQuotationList: ['At Aunt Agatha s. I have asked myself and Mr. Gray. He is her latest\n_prot g _.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText:
Humph! tell your Aunt Agatha, Harry, not to bother me any more with
her charity appeals. I am sick of them. Why, the good woman thinks that
I have nothing to do but to write cheques for her silly fads.
subQuotationList: ['Humph! tell your Aunt Agatha, Harry, not to bother me any more with\nher charity appeals. I am sick of them. Why, the good woman thinks that\nI have nothing to do but to write cheques for her silly fads.']
speaker: Lord Fermor
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
All right, Uncle George, I ll tell her, but it won t have any effect.
Philanthropic people lose all sense of humanity. It is their
distinguishing characteristic.
subQuotationList: ['All right, Uncle George, I ll tell her, but it won t have any effect.\nPhilanthropic people lose all sense of humanity. It is their\ndistinguishing characteristic.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Lord Fermor']

quoteText: Late as usual, Harry,
subQuotationList: ['Late as usual, Harry,']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
We are talking about poor Dartmoor, Lord Henry, Do you think he will
really marry this fascinating young person?
subQuotationList: ['We are talking about poor Dartmoor, Lord Henry,', 'Do you think he will\nreally marry this fascinating young person?']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon'], ['Lord Henry']], [['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText: I believe she has made up her mind to propose to him, Duchess.
subQuotationList: ['I believe she has made up her mind to propose to him, Duchess.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
How dreadful! Really, some one should
interfere.
subQuotationList: ['How dreadful!', 'Really, some one should\ninterfere.']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
I am told, on excellent authority, that her father keeps an American
dry-goods store,
subQuotationList: ['I am told, on excellent authority, that her father keeps an American\ndry-goods store,']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText: My uncle has already suggested pork-packing, Sir Thomas.
subQuotationList: ['My uncle has already suggested pork-packing, Sir Thomas.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Dry-goods! What are American dry-goods?
subQuotationList: ['Dry-goods! What are American dry-goods?']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: American novels,
subQuotationList: ['American novels,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Don t mind him, my dear, He never means
anything that he says.
subQuotationList: ['Don t mind him, my dear,', 'He never means\nanything that he says.']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: When America was discovered,
subQuotationList: ['When America was discovered,']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
I wish to goodness it never had been
discovered at all! Really, our girls have no chance
nowadays. It is most unfair.
subQuotationList: ['I wish to goodness it never had been\ndiscovered at all!', 'Really, our girls have no chance\nnowadays. It is most unfair.']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Perhaps, after all, America never has been discovered, I myself would say that it had merely been detected.
subQuotationList: ['Perhaps, after all, America never has been discovered,', 'I myself would say that it had merely been detected.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Oh! but I have seen specimens of the inhabitants, I must confess that most of them are extremely
pretty. And they dress well, too. They get all their dresses in Paris.
I wish I could afford to do the same.
subQuotationList: ['Oh! but I have seen specimens of the inhabitants,', 'I must confess that most of them are extremely\npretty. And they dress well, too. They get all their dresses in Paris.\nI wish I could afford to do the same.']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: They say that when good Americans die they go to Paris,
subQuotationList: ['They say that when good Americans die they go to Paris,']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText: Really! And where do bad Americans go to when they die?
subQuotationList: ['Really! And where do bad Americans go to when they die?']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: They go to America,
subQuotationList: ['They go to America,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
I am afraid that your nephew is prejudiced against
that great country, I have travelled all over
it in cars provided by the directors, who, in such matters, are
extremely civil. I assure you that it is an education to visit it.
subQuotationList:
['I am afraid that your nephew is prejudiced against
that great country,', 'I have travelled all over
it in cars provided by the directors, who, in such matters, are
extremely civil. I assure you that it is an education to visit it.']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText: But must we really see Chicago in order to be educated? I don t feel up to the journey.
subQuotationList: ['But must we really see Chicago in order to be educated?', 'I don t feel up to the journey.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Mr. Erskine of Treadley has the world on
his shelves. We practical men like to see things, not to read about
them. The Americans are an extremely interesting people. They are
absolutely reasonable. I think that is their distinguishing
characteristic. Yes, Mr. Erskine, an absolutely reasonable people. I
assure you there is no nonsense about the Americans.
subQuotationList:
['Mr. Erskine of Treadley has the world on
his shelves. We practical men like to see things, not to read about
them. The Americans are an extremely interesting people. They are
absolutely reasonable. I think that is their distinguishing
characteristic. Yes, Mr. Erskine, an absolutely reasonable people. I
assure you there is no nonsense about the Americans.']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
How dreadful! I can stand brute force, but brute
reason is quite unbearable. There is something unfair about its use. It
is hitting below the intellect.
subQuotationList: ['How dreadful!', 'I can stand brute force, but brute\nreason is quite unbearable. There is something unfair about its use. It\nis hitting below the intellect.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: I do not understand you,
subQuotationList: ['I do not understand you,']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText: I do, Lord Henry,
subQuotationList: ['I do, Lord Henry,']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Paradoxes are all very well in their way....
subQuotationList: ['Paradoxes are all very well in their way....']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
Was that a paradox? I did not think so. Perhaps
it was. Well, the way of paradoxes is the way of truth. To test reality
we must see it on the tight rope. When the verities become acrobats, we
can judge them.
subQuotationList:
['Was that a paradox?', 'I did not think so. Perhaps
it was. Well, the way of paradoxes is the way of truth. To test reality
we must see it on the tight rope. When the verities become acrobats, we
can judge them.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Dear me! how you men argue! I am sure I never can
make out what you are talking about. Oh! Harry, I am quite vexed with
you. Why do you try to persuade our nice Mr. Dorian Gray to give up the
East End? I assure you he would be quite invaluable. They would love
his playing.
subQuotationList:
['Dear me!', 'how you men argue! I am sure I never can
make out what you are talking about. Oh! Harry, I am quite vexed with
you. Why do you try to persuade our nice Mr. Dorian Gray to give up the
East End? I assure you he would be quite invaluable. They would love
his playing.']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionTextsList: [[], ['you men', 'you', 'Harry', 'you', 'you', 'our nice Mr. Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Dorian ', 'you', 'he', 'They', 'his']]
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon'], ['Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: I want him to play to me,
subQuotationList: ['I want him to play to me,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: But they are so unhappy in Whitechapel,
subQuotationList: ['But they are so unhappy in Whitechapel,']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
I can sympathize with everything except suffering, I cannot sympathize with that. It is too
ugly, too horrible, too distressing. There is something terribly morbid
in the modern sympathy with pain. One should sympathize with the
colour, the beauty, the joy of life. The less said about life s sores,
the better.
subQuotationList:
['I can sympathize with everything except suffering,', 'I cannot sympathize with that. It is too
ugly, too horrible, too distressing. There is something terribly morbid
in the modern sympathy with pain. One should sympathize with the
colour, the beauty, the joy of life. The less said about life s sores,
the better.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Still, the East End is a very important problem,
subQuotationList: ['Still, the East End is a very important problem,']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
Quite so, It is the problem of slavery, and
we try to solve it by amusing the slaves.
subQuotationList: ['Quite so,', 'It is the problem of slavery, and\nwe try to solve it by amusing the slaves.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Dorian Gray', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Henry', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']]]

quoteText:
What change do you propose,
then?
subQuotationList: ['What change do you propose,\nthen?']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
I don t desire to change anything in England
except the weather, I am quite content with philosophic
contemplation. But, as the nineteenth century has gone bankrupt through
an over-expenditure of sympathy, I would suggest that we should appeal
to science to put us straight. The advantage of the emotions is that
they lead us astray, and the advantage of science is that it is not
emotional.
subQuotationList:
['I don t desire to change anything in England
except the weather,', 'I am quite content with philosophic
contemplation. But, as the nineteenth century has gone bankrupt through
an over-expenditure of sympathy, I would suggest that we should appeal
to science to put us straight. The advantage of the emotions is that
they lead us astray, and the advantage of science is that it is not
emotional.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: But we have such grave responsibilities,
subQuotationList: ['But we have such grave responsibilities,']
speaker: Mrs. Vandeleur
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Terribly grave,
subQuotationList: ['Terribly grave,']
speaker: Lady Agatha
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Humanity takes itself too
seriously. It is the world s original sin. If the caveman had known how
to laugh, history would have been different.
subQuotationList: ['Humanity takes itself too\nseriously. It is the world s original sin. If the caveman had known how\nto laugh, history would have been different.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
You are really very comforting, I have always
felt rather guilty when I came to see your dear aunt, for I take no
interest at all in the East End. For the future I shall be able to look
her in the face without a blush.
subQuotationList:
['You are really very comforting,', 'I have always
felt rather guilty when I came to see your dear aunt, for I take no
interest at all in the East End. For the future I shall be able to look
her in the face without a blush.']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: A blush is very becoming, Duchess,
subQuotationList: ['A blush is very becoming, Duchess,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Only when one is young, When an old woman like myself
blushes, it is a very bad sign. Ah! Lord Henry, I wish you would tell
me how to become young again.
subQuotationList: ['Only when one is young,', 'When an old woman like myself\nblushes, it is a very bad sign. Ah! Lord Henry, I wish you would tell\nme how to become young again.']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Can you remember any great error that you
committed in your early days, Duchess?
subQuotationList: ['Can you remember any great error that you\ncommitted in your early days, Duchess?']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Duchess Of Harley'], ['Duchess Of Harley'], ['Duchess Of Harley'], ['Duchess Of Harley']]]

quoteText: A great many, I fear,
subQuotationList: ['A great many, I fear,']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Then commit them over again, To get back one s
youth, one has merely to repeat one s follies.
subQuotationList: ['Then commit them over again,', 'To get back one s\nyouth, one has merely to repeat one s follies.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: A delightful theory! I must put it into practice.
subQuotationList: ['A delightful theory!', 'I must put it into practice.']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: A dangerous theory!
subQuotationList: ['A dangerous theory!']
speaker: Sir Thomas Burdon
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Duchess Of Harley']

quoteText:
Yes, that is one of the great secrets of life.
Nowadays most people die of a sort of creeping common sense, and
discover when it is too late that the only things one never regrets are
one s mistakes.
subQuotationList:
['Yes,', 'that is one of the great secrets of life.
Nowadays most people die of a sort of creeping common sense, and
discover when it is too late that the only things one never regrets are
one s mistakes.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
How annoying! I must go. I have to call for my husband at the club, to take
him to some absurd meeting at Willis s Rooms, where he is going to be
in the chair. If I am late he is sure to be furious, and I couldn t
have a scene in this bonnet. It is far too fragile. A harsh word would
ruin it. No, I must go, dear Agatha. Good-bye, Lord Henry, you are
quite delightful and dreadfully demoralizing. I am sure I don t know
what to say about your views. You must come and dine with us some
night. Tuesday? Are you disengaged Tuesday?
subQuotationList:
['How annoying!', 'I must go. I have to call for my husband at the club, to take
him to some absurd meeting at Willis s Rooms, where he is going to be
in the chair. If I am late he is sure to be furious, and I couldn t
have a scene in this bonnet. It is far too fragile. A harsh word would

quite delightful and dreadfully demoralizing. I am sure I don t know
what to say about your views. You must come and dine with us some
night. Tuesday? Are you disengaged Tuesday?']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Lady Agatha'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Duchess Of Harley'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText: For you I would throw over anybody, Duchess,
subQuotationList: ['For you I would throw over anybody, Duchess,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Ah! that is very nice, and very wrong of you, so mind you
come
subQuotationList: ['Ah! that is very nice, and very wrong of you,', 'so mind you\ncome']
speaker: Duchess Of Harley
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: You talk books away, why don t you write one?
subQuotationList: ['You talk books away,', 'why don t you write one?']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
I am too fond of reading books to care to write them, Mr. Erskine. I
should like to write a novel certainly, a novel that would be as lovely
as a Persian carpet and as unreal. But there is no literary public in
England for anything except newspapers, primers, and encyclopaedias. Of
all people in the world the English have the least sense of the beauty
of literature.
subQuotationList:
['I am too fond of reading books to care to write them, Mr. Erskine. I
should like to write a novel certainly, a novel that would be as lovely
as a Persian carpet and as unreal. But there is no literary public in
England for anything except newspapers, primers, and encyclopaedias. Of
all people in the world the English have the least sense of the beauty
of literature.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
I fear you are right, I myself used to have
literary ambitions, but I gave them up long ago. And now, my dear young
friend, if you will allow me to call you so, may I ask if you really
meant all that you said to us at lunch?
subQuotationList:
['I fear you are right,', 'I myself used to have
literary ambitions, but I gave them up long ago. And now, my dear young
friend, if you will allow me to call you so, may I ask if you really
meant all that you said to us at lunch?']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry']], [['Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Mrs. Vandeleur', 'Lady Agatha', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Duchess Of Harley', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']]]

quoteText: I quite forget what I said, Was it all very bad?
subQuotationList: ['I quite forget what I said,', 'Was it all very bad?']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Very bad indeed. In fact I consider you extremely dangerous, and if
anything happens to our good duchess, we shall all look on you as being
primarily responsible. But I should like to talk to you about life. The
generation into which I was born was tedious. Some day, when you are
tired of London, come down to Treadley and expound to me your
philosophy of pleasure over some admirable Burgundy I am fortunate
enough to possess.
subQuotationList:
['Very bad indeed. In fact I consider you extremely dangerous, and if
anything happens to our good duchess, we shall all look on you as being
primarily responsible. But I should like to talk to you about life. The
generation into which I was born was tedious. Some day, when you are
tired of London, come down to Treadley and expound to me your
philosophy of pleasure over some admirable Burgundy I am fortunate
enough to possess.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry'], ['Duchess Of Harley'], ['Mr. Erskine'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
I shall be charmed. A visit to Treadley would be a great privilege. It
has a perfect host, and a perfect library.
subQuotationList: ['I shall be charmed. A visit to Treadley would be a great privilege. It\nhas a perfect host, and a perfect library.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
You will complete it, And now I must bid good-bye to your excellent aunt. I am due at
the Athenaeum. It is the hour when we sleep there.
subQuotationList: ['You will complete it,', 'And now I must bid good-bye to your excellent aunt. I am due at\nthe Athenaeum. It is the hour when we sleep there.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: All of you, Mr. Erskine?
subQuotationList: ['All of you, Mr. Erskine?']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText:
Forty of us, in forty arm-chairs. We are practising for an English
Academy of Letters.
subQuotationList: ['Forty of us, in forty arm-chairs. We are practising for an English\nAcademy of Letters.']
speaker: Mr. Erskine
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: I am going to the park,
subQuotationList: ['I am going to the park,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray', 'Mr. Erskine', 'Sir Thomas Burdon']

quoteText: Let me come with you,
subQuotationList: ['Let me come with you,']
speaker: Dorian Gray
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText: But I thought you had promised Basil Hallward to go and see him,
subQuotationList: ['But I thought you had promised Basil Hallward to go and see him,']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray']

quoteText:
I would sooner come with you; yes, I feel I must come with you. Do let
me. And you will promise to talk to me all the time? No one talks so
wonderfully as you do.
subQuotationList: ['I would sooner come with you; yes, I feel I must come with you. Do let\nme. And you will promise to talk to me all the time? No one talks so\nwonderfully as you do.']
speaker: Dorian Gray
addressees: ['Lord Henry']

quoteText:
Ah! I have talked quite enough for to-day, All I want now is to look at life. You may come and look at it with
me, if you care to.
subQuotationList: ['Ah! I have talked quite enough for to-day,', 'All I want now is to look at life. You may come and look at it with\nme, if you care to.']
speaker: Lord Henry
addressees: ['Dorian Gray']
